summary: The Office of the Inspector General for the NYPD released a report using statistical analysis to demonstrate that 'Broken Windows' policing doesn't reduce felony crime. The six-year study found no correlation between quality-of-life summonses or misdemeanor arrests and drops in felony crime. In fact, as quality-of-life enforcement dramatically declined between 2010-2015, felony crime did not increase. The report recommends the NYPD take a more data-driven approach and analyze whether their enforcement disproportionately impacts minorities and young males. The NYPD has called the report flawed and promised a response within 90 days.
key_points: OIG-NYPD found no statistical evidence linking quality-of-life enforcement (summonses, misdemeanor arrests) to the drop in felony crime over six years
Quality-of-life summons rates dramatically declined between 2010-2015 with no corresponding increase in felony crime
The report recommends analyzing whether enforcement disproportionately impacts black and Hispanic residents, males aged 15-20, and NYCHA residents
The NYPD disputes the findings and will respond within 90 days
money_quotes: OIG-NYPD found no evidence that the drop in felony crime observed over the past six years was related to quality-of-life summonses or quality-of-life misdemeanor arrests.
Whatever has contributed to the observed drop in felony crime remains an open question worthy of further analysis.
The report goes on to say that the NYPD should take a more data driven approach to deciding what's actually working and what isn't.
Very happy about this report, it's been a super long time coming.
key_insight: Statistical analysis reveals that the foundational assumption of Broken Windows policing—that aggressive enforcement of minor violations reduces serious crime—has no empirical support, making the case for data-driven reform of police practices.
